ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic kebab food product class; no specific vegetable kebab platter entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Grilled vegetables) is the closest defensible code for a grilled mixed-vegetable skewer dish. No specific vegetable kebab platter code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for vegetable kebab platter; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (bell peppers, tomatoes, onions, zucchini, mushrooms)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard vegetable kebab platter serving is approximately 250 g, comprising several skewers of grilled mixed vegetables with accompanying sauces or chutneys.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vegetable Kebab Platter (cr_id G00010841). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated at 6.5 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central reference for grilled mixed vegetables with oil and yogurt marinade (USDA SR Legacy, mixed vegetable dish with added fat). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and water not reported by HPB; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed grilled vegetables with spices
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vegetable Kebab Platter. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for mixed grilled non-starchy vegetables with spice marinade. Non-starchy vegetables typically have GI 15–55 depending on preparation; grilling with oil and spice marinade moderates glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (13.1 g for 250 g portion) / 100 = 5.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100 g — critically elevated, nearly double the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or severely restrict this dish; sodium at 1080 mg/100 g far exceeds safe limits, and even a 100 g portion delivers 54% of the 2000 mg daily sodium target — request reduced-salt preparation and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as chutneys.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100 g) and low GI (~45) are favourable, but high fat (26.4 g/100 g) and zero dietary fibre are concerns for insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate load and estimated low GI make this dish acceptable for blood glucose management in moderate portions, but the high fat content (26.4 g/100 g) may impair insulin sensitivity over time — pair with a high-fibre side dish and limit portion to 150 g or less.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100 g, total fat 26.4 g/100 g, estimated saturated fat 6.5 g/100 g, and cholesterol 85 mg/100 g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish poses a high cardiovascular risk due to critically elevated sodium (1080 mg/100 g), high total fat, and significant saturated fat and cholesterol — individuals with CVD or dyslipidaemia should limit consumption to small portions and request low-oil, low-salt preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold; nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, tomatoes) may trigger joint inflammation; estimated saturated fat 6.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish or consume only very small portions with reduced-salt preparation; the critically high sodium worsens tissue swelling, nightshade ingredients may provoke joint flares, and elevated saturated fat promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Kebab Platter is a grilled mixed-vegetable dish served as a platter, popular in Singapore's Indian and Turkish fusion food scene. The dish consists of assorted non-starchy vegetables — typically bell peppers, tomatoes, onions, zucchini, and mushrooms — threaded onto skewers, marinated in a spiced yogurt-based mixture containing cumin, coriander, chili, and turmeric, then grilled over high heat until lightly charred and tender. The result is a smoky, aromatic dish with a slightly crisp exterior and juicy interior, carrying bold Indian and Turkish spice flavours. Nutritionally, the dish is high in fat (26.4 g/100 g) and sodium (1080 mg/100 g), with moderate protein (10.7 g/100 g) and low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100 g). Dietary fibre is reported as zero, which is atypical for a vegetable-dominant dish and likely reflects the HPB source measurement for this specific preparation. Cholesterol is 85 mg/100 g, suggesting the marinade includes dairy or egg components. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable. Clinically, the critically high sodium content makes it unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management without significant modification. The low GI (~45) and low carbohydrate load are favourable for glycaemic control, but the high fat content warrants caution for individuals managing insulin resistance or dyslipidaemia. Best consumed in moderate portions alongside high-fibre accompaniments.
common_user_queries: Is vegetable kebab good for diabetics?
Can I eat vegetable kebab with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id G00010841, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous grilled vegetable dish with added fat. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous non-starchy grilled vegetable entry. All values are internally consistent and plausible for the described preparation.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this specific preparation — this is confirmed zero per the HPB source, though it is atypical for a vegetable-dominant dish; the zero value likely reflects the specific commercial preparation measured. Saturated fat (6.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference for grilled mixed vegetables with oil and yogurt marinade, as HPB did not report saturated fat breakdown. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and moisture data for this specific vegetable kebab preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond the HPB-reported panel is not available in the source record. The input food_group 'Meat and alternatives' and food_subgroup 'Other meats' are preserved from the HPB source classification but are semantically imprecise for a vegetable-only dish; food_subgroup has been updated to 'Vegetable-based dishes' for semantic accuracy. Cholesterol at 85 mg/100 g is preserved from HPB and suggests the marinade contains dairy or egg components. The cuisine classification 'Western' in the input is corrected to 'Indian / Turkish fusion (Singapore hawker adaptation)' based on the dish description.
